transcript.transcript: Okay. You were going to tell me a little bit about flooding in Bonner's Ferry now. Yes, but I left it, to refer to Mother and Father on the early collection. I've heard it many times, but I wasn't born at that time. The biggest flood ever recorded in Bonner's Ferry was 1894. The water was so high that it overthrew the banks of the town, causing the shipwreck to come in from behind, spreading over the entire city. Small clean buildings were caught up in the current, causing them to wash against other houses. The rest of the reservoirs in the entire town were there. They washed away. Everyone took to the hills above the railroad tracks. Tents were put up for the women and children, and rowboats were getting people to lie down. People living in two-story houses moved upstairs. Others moved to the upper floors of the hotels. The railroad tracks were flooded. There was five feet of water on the main street, grocery stocks were bogged, folks were hired to hire ground, everybody got groceries, money or not. Many buildings on the river bank were washed down rivers, others collapsed. It was a real dirty patch, We unlocked everything, home and all the contents, but the more important one shared with the unfortunate. People were like that nowadays. And then we faced Guam for 1903. But the 1903 flood was not quite in the town area. The entire town was under water, back in from the lower side. The current year remained in the main channel preventing the dam from being damaged. In 1894, the Florida flood dam, most people lived upstairs, of course cooked meals downstairs, had stove tops and still a lot of water, and rubber boots we needed to flop around into the fair and eat on. We served upstairs. We'd catch may grass and pulled our way around town, and during the end of the boat, we got boats. We like it. 1915 was the reputation of the 1903. The river arose slowly, giving people time to prepare, moving toilets and so on up. Grocery stores put everything up on shelves and benches out of reach of the water. They didn't do it on just the train, they were involved. Naturally, the wood type office had to be anchored and it was pulled off. The big door after it was plugged was shut and they went from the houses. In 1948 another flood and the dikes broke through the town and they watered back in. This project is more of a picnic than any other. It's probably about a foot on my feet. You mentioned the dikes there. Approximately, how many dikes did you get involved? Well, I would give you a clear picture of the Kootenai down. That's practically 25 miles to the boundary line, Canadian boundary line. And you mentioned both sides of the river, large meadows and lakes. It was a very rich ground. So there's been talk for years of dyking and draining the lake, so this man can be fine. Well, my father in 1923 got a contract with a man named Martin Blosman in Spokane to take the bond to dike District Number 1, as it was called, being the first district that was called that, District Number 1. That had become a foreign prize that's been accepted. Other districts assumed the foreign prize, District 1 and 2, District 3. Other gifts get tuned for them, and different ones who receive better. Both Dyson and Drain, Drain, this land sold for $5. Now before Dyson and Drain, this land sold for $5, the rate was, sold for $5. Now, before Dykengreen, this man sold for $5 per acre. Present price, $375 to $400 per acre, and very little for sale. The yield of wheat, oats, and seeds, copper, were tremendous. I can talk to you about that. Mr. Kent, do you remember your father telling you any particular stories about his life? Well, yes. In 1893, he decided to go down in Canada with another man by the name of Owens, Cotting. They were in the Kootenai Lake and their plans were to go up the Largo River and establish a camp. They left Bonner's Ferry in a Peter Burroughs canoe and went all the way down and crossed the lake and then pulled the canoe up to Largo River, which is quite a few miles, and establish camp and build a cabin for the winter. And they do the trapping. Well, they had a very successful winter in trapping, but they should've came out earlier. They went going, wanted to stay and get a few bear when they come out of hibernation. But their bear prices were good. So Dad consented to stay with them. But in the meantime, it turned very, very warm for this time of year. And the Largo River started to raise. They were successful in getting a few bears, but at the time they were going to go, The river was up and the snow was melting fast. But they lost their canoe and all their furs and guns, and they had a dog with them. The guards were a little bit suspicious about getting down there. It might be in the swamp. So they took off their shoes and they tied them to the canoe in case they were in a swamp, they would hang up someplace and they could recover their shoes. A lake started down and wasn't going long until they hit a tree burst. It was a tree that fell across the river in a swamp and dad was put into the water and the dog and the drawing but he didn't see where Mr. Owens went and I guess Mr. Owens didn't see which way dad went and circled the path and never did see the dog again. But dad got out on one side of the largo, no shoes, no guns, no nothing. And started for the lake. To the lake. Now, by this time the lake was up and Bonner's Ferry was flying. And there was a Jew, by the name of Balford, that had run out on the point of Creek New Lake and then the water raised to the highest and washed this out of the building. And when he hit down at the bottom of this soft piece of the lake right here, he was fortunate enough to find a boat that was washed away. And he and, well, there was one paddle in the boat. An oar. Well he got in that boat and started up toward Bonner's Ferry in the lake. And as he was paddling along he noticed a man come down to the lake and go back up into the hills. So he put in there and he went up and there was an old man with a trapper's cabin. And Dad told him what had happened. So he fixed him up with food and he left him up on that ferry. And they whittled out another oar for the boat. So that took them three days to go to length to the lake and a hundred miles up the river in one blinding day. But in the meantime, Mr. Owen had got back to Bonner's Ferry. Of course, Mother was being alone down there when the flood came, but businessmen and neighbors like both her and a few children up on the hill in a tent. Nobody to help her but them. She was wondering about that and her husband, of course. She was in the tent one morning and she heard somebody kind of sobbing outside and she says, well, who's there? He answered, this is Owen's Mrs. Kent. And she said, well, where's my husband? He said, oh, I hate to tell you, but Andy was drowned. And, first mother fainted. About twice she quit on fainting anyway. And, and, went around for a couple days and everybody was sympathizing with her and the business men were trying to get her onto the train and boat to go back to her mother in Pullman. But the day she was going to leave, she wasn't in the tent, she had a socks back that was worn. And surely knowing she looked around and understood. I was wearing a patched back that was worn, and I'm sure you know him, he looked around and there was Sidney. Saw the dirty hat on him, a smile on his face, and a shallow-tailed coat that he had picked up. A shoe of a different kind on each foot. And she paid him again. And she paid him again. By the congression of Dale. But they lost everything. Guns, dogs, canoes. That was the 94th time it got there. Mr. Kent, your father, A.J. Kent, was very instrumental in both local and state politics wasn't he? Oh yes he was for years he was county chairman of the Republican Party. And he was an unfriendly friend of the United States senators, and Senator Borrell was an instrument and an outlet for the Cooney Valley in controlling the water in the Springs District, carried efforts with Canada. And in 1921 and 1922, he was elected state legislator for Oregon. And in 1924, he carried electoral votes back to Oregon with his team for Cougney. And in 1924 he carried a lectorial work back to work on the D.C. Part, including... I think it was... He was still acted on politics right up to Idaho. It was 1940. Thank you very much. This case will be placed in the library at North Idaho College for use by future researchers. I hate the wind like this.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the most significant flood in 1894, describing how it overwhelmed the town, causing extensive damage and displacement. Buildings were washed away, and the community had to adapt quickly, moving to higher ground and changing their living arrangements to cope with the floodwaters. The narrative captures the community's resilience and the drastic measures they took to survive during such catastrophic events.

metadata.title: Historical Floods and Their Impact
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the most significant flood in 1894, describing how it overwhelmed the town, causing extensive damage and displacement. Buildings were washed away, and the community had to adapt quickly, moving to higher ground and changing their living arrangements to cope with the floodwaters. The narrative captures the community's resilience and the drastic measures they took to survive during such catastrophic events.

lines.line: > The speaker explains how the Dutch, known for their expertise in dyking and drainage, influenced land management in the area. This knowledge was crucial in transforming previously unmanageable land into fertile farmland, significantly increasing its value and productivity. The speaker's father played a role in these developments, highlighting the long-term benefits of such land management techniques.

metadata.title: Dutch Influence on Land Management
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains how the Dutch, known for their expertise in dyking and drainage, influenced land management in the area. This knowledge was crucial in transforming previously unmanageable land into fertile farmland, significantly increasing its value and productivity. The speaker's father played a role in these developments, highlighting the long-term benefits of such land management techniques.

lines.line: # Adventures and Survival of the Speaker's Father

> The speaker recounts a harrowing story of his father's trapping expedition with a partner, which turned perilous due to an unexpected flood. The narrative details their struggle for survival after losing their canoe and supplies, and the eventual miraculous reunion with the community. This personal story underscores the unpredictability of nature and the resilience required to overcome such challenges.

metadata.title: Adventures and Survival of the Speaker's Father

lines.line: # Political Contributions of the Speaker's Father

> The speaker discusses his father's significant role in politics, particularly his influence within the Republican Party and his contributions to regional water management and legislative actions. His political career is portrayed as impactful, with connections that extended nationally, and his legacy continued to influence local politics even after his death.
